All right, welcome back. So in keeping with this whole idea of getting more money, what we're gonna do now is we're gonna take a deep dive in the one-to-many sales process, which is when you make a pitch to a whole bunch of people, thusly one-to-many. And specifically, this is in regards to using a webinar. This could be a live webinar. It could be a recorded webinar. It could be an on-demand class, which is a fancy way of saying the video that someone opts in to see. It doesn't really matter, right? As long as you're getting a whole bunch of people to look at a thing that makes a pitch, that's what we're talking about. Now, specifically with this one, we're talking about using this to sell a thing where the person actually orders directly online. Now, some webinars, like the ones that we use to sell our agency services, for example, people that actually buy online, they actually fill out an application. That's an entirely different process altogether. And naturally, we're going to cover how to make that one work even better as well than this week. But what this one is all about is how to do a webinar sales process correctly in terms of taking it and making it significantly more profitable. Now, one thing I want to share with you is what I'm going to show you is the way to trick one of these things out as much as humanly possible. So I'm going to show you like, dude, here's all the really, really hardcore things that you can do in terms of email sequences to people who attend, but they leave early, and people who see the pitch, but don't buy, and people who start to buy, and then don't finish their cart, and all of that kind of stuff. You might look at this process and be like, oh my gosh, dude, that's a ton of moving pieces. If it starts to feel overwhelming to you, and you're like, man, I don't want to do all of that kind of stuff, I would suggest cutting out the part where you actually track whether or not someone stays long enough to see the pitch and treat all attendees equally. Now, that's not necessarily the best way to do it, but if you're in a hurry and you're like, okay, I don't want to have it be this complicated, that's one little thing that you could shave off and it won't hurt you too bad. Personally, I suggest you follow the process map to the letter because it makes, what did they say? Moe Money, that's what they said in the hippity hop music, Moe Money, which does not, as I've stated earlier, it does not at all lead to Moe problems. So speaking of Moe Money, let's walk you through how to get Moe Money from a webinar sales process. Here we go. By one to many, in this case, I mean, a live webinar, or one of them automated webinars, or one of them on-demand classes, or one of them long-form VSLs. Some of the components to what I'm gonna show you could potentially apply to a one to many, like in-person, physical event-based process, but not really, all right? So you're familiar with webinars and all of this kind of stuff, all right? Let me ask you a question. So aside from, you know, like obviously selling stuff, what's the biggest deal? Okay, what's most important here? Registrants, all right? How many people are signing up? Attendees, how many people see the damn thing? Or number three, how many people see the pitch? Well, what's interesting is you probably already know, because of my tonality, because I can't really, I don't have a good poker face or a poker voice, but it's the offer, right? How many people see the offer? Most of your clients, if they're doing a one to many presentation, whether it's webinar or on-demand class or whatever, they're gonna focus on these numbers right here, all right? But the real money in this thing is getting more people to see the offer more frequently because the offer is what makes the money. You know, so if I've got a great webinar and it gives all of this good content and at the end of it I pitch and 99% of the people show up, but none of them see the pitch, then matter, right? And that's where the thing is broken. And if I'm the client and I don't know these types of things, I might think the entire webinar itself is broken or on-demand class or whatever, right? So here's what most of your clients that do this, most of them that are running one to many type things, I'm just gonna kind of use webinars, the general example, although the framework is the same. Typically, regardless of company size, this is what's going on. They get a registrant and then they're like, did they attend? And if they didn't attend, they send them the replay. If they did attend, they're like, did they buy the thing? And if they didn't buy, they send them the replay. And then if they still don't buy, hopefully if it's your client, hopefully we get lucky and they do something really dumb like, stick them in a nurture sequence. And that's just where they're, you know, in the general list and they get random promotions with no real structure. This is kind of like what 99% of all one to many style sales processes entail. You know, it's, you got the main thing and then you got the replay and they either buy it or they don't. And some folks will get a little jiggy with it. And if it's an automated webinar, they'll send the indoctrination videos and all that kind of stuff because they studied my stuff years ago and then they didn't keep up with the times and know that we don't have to do that anymore, you know? So there's a better way. So what I wanna do for you right now is I wanna show you how to take one of these if your client has one of these. I wanna show you all the cool stuff that could be done with one of these. And then I wanna give you the checklist so you can see what parts your clients are missing and then just prescribe the one thing as usual. So as we talked about the other week, you know, there's four types of businesses generally and they've all got some processes that are relatively standard issue for their businesses. So let me zoom out for us a little bit here. Okay, you can see this is kind of a big process map. There's really not a lot to this. But first, let me just sort of walk you through what this really should look like. So in most cases, and this is funny, all right? So you've got a client and your client will have an email list and they'll be doing two things. They'll be running ads to a webinar, right? I've replaced my decision diamonds with these bigger things because it's easier to read. I know technically speaking for all of you people who are actually good at process maps, that's not a decision diamond, whatever, humor me. My eyesight's bad. Anyway, your client might run ads to a registration page and then they've got an email list and they might even like run email campaigns telling their list to go register for the webinar, okay? So the very first thing that we've gotta remember is if you've got a client that's doing this, that's emailing their own list to go register, which is opt in again, even though you've already got the gosh dang contact data in order to go and see the webinar, all we're doing is putting a big barrier between that person seeing the offer, right? And so just as a general rule, it's never a good idea to make someone go and register for a webinar if they're already on the client's list. Unless there's a specific purpose. So for example, we have people that use the phones in my company, so if I don't have a contact's phone number, I'll tell them to go register for a webinar even though they're on my list because I'll ask for the phone number at point of registration. It was a general rule that we can immediately say, oh dude, okay, well if we remove the need to register and just tell them to go watch the webinar itself, then they're good. So let me explain, and this doesn't necessarily have that much to do with this process map, but let me explain kind of the new deal in terms of one too many. As you know, a huge amount of traffic, especially ad-driven traffic comes from social media, right? So imagine this situation, which is a situation that happens every day. Somebody sees an ad for a webinar or whatever on Facebook, they go register, the registration page says, okay, what's starting just happens to be starting in 15 minutes or whatever. So now they register and they fill out their email form, so now they've left and they're usually doing it on their phone, so now they've left the Facebook, gone and registered and now they have to go check their email and then from their email, they've gotta go and click on the link to go watch the video, right? So that's like this type of situation here. You can almost universally, immediately make things better by not doing that and just having what's called an on-demand class, which is somebody registers and the thank you page is the actual video slash webinar thingy itself. They don't have to wait and all of the attendance and all that stuff can still be tracked, so that's kind of a little hack there. On-demand classes convert way better in my experience than webinars. So that's what this means by the way, a go watch sequence. Any CRM, whether it's Infusionsoft or whatever, if it's set up properly, you can send somebody a link to a video and actually track how long they watch that video and tag them accordingly, meaning know if they stayed for five minutes or if they saw the pitch or whatever, right? So regardless, like here are kind of the three ways someone could be in, get themselves into a one-to-many presentation, whether it's webinar on-demand class or whatever. So in this case, here's like, notice I've got stars in this process map. The stars are things that could immediately move the needle for somebody. So a liquidator is a cheap little product, right? Some folks would call it a tripwire, little $5 ebook or whatever, just a little thing. Okay, so the addition of that little thing, which is essentially the framework I gave you last week, by the way, the addition of that sales letter that has an order form with an order bump and then it's got some one-click up sales, that thing after the registration, but before seeing the webinar itself, can immediately move the dial for your client. Just sticking one of those in there. If it's so few people do it, including myself, by the way, as I make these materials for you, I'm like, oh my God, I'm so dumb, I never follow my own advice. So just adding that is very, very significant in terms of helping. And don't worry, I'm gonna give you a checklist that kind of covers all of this stuff later. I just wanna go into how this stuff really can be made exceptional. All right, so thing number one, run ads or send emails or whatever, they're go to the registration page. I have sent you a link to the training. Meanwhile, please read this very important letter and then boom, there's the pitch. Or if it's a video, you know, and we've got tools for all of this, by the way, it's not tool time yet. If they wanna do a video, it's, hey, I got your registration, there's an email on the way to you, but before you watch this training, I wanna tell you about something really, really special. Here's the pitch, right? That's a liquidator offer. Okay, so next thing we wanna do in this case is you wanna make sure that somebody has attended. So whether it's an automated webinar and on-demand class, our first checkpoint here is to see if they attended, right? Like that's the big deal. Now your client is likely, if they don't attend, just go watch the replay and there's gonna be a couple of emails to the replay. What I recommend is they switch from all that automated webinar stuff to just doing on-demand trainings and on-demand classes because let's say they opt in here, right? They buy the liquidator or they don't and they end up on the page that plays the video, which is the thing they opted in for. That page can track whether or not they stayed for five minutes. And if they did not, now the five minute mark, by the way, is my definition of having actually attended. They don't stay for five minutes. I don't count them as having attended. If they didn't, you got a couple of things that could be deployed, right? Number one is a sequence that says, hey, you left early, go back and watch it. Now, if you're doing on-demand classes, then they can pick up where they left off. Totally different than the replay. It's got a totally different vibe. You can use completely different language in this sequence. I've got a framework for the sequence for you as well and tools and all of that kind of stuff. Again, this is 30,000 foot view. Okay, so as we go through the checklist here in a minute, later on in this video of like, here's all the stuff we wanna look and see if they got. If they don't have a go back sequence and if they're not measuring attendance, then instant, instant needle mover, right? Why would that be? Well, because they can't see the offer. This is the most valuable part of the webinar, by the way, seeing the pitch, right? They can't see the pitch if they don't get to at least the first five minutes. So we have to have language that drives them to that first five minutes. So let's say that they either attend immediately or they're in the, hey, go watch it sequence and they finally go here and they attend. Well, now what we need to do is in the system, right? In the campaign, we need to wait. Let's say that the webinar is an hour and the pitch is at the 50 minute mark, okay? So we need to wait for at least 51 minutes to see whether or not that person sees a pitch. Now, you don't need to be tech savvy to do this sort of thing. Your client, they've already got a person. And even if they don't, let's say they're using Infusionsoft, if you just go to Google and type in Infusionsoft certified partners and hand them this thing and this video, they'll know how to handle it. So it's not like you need to physically do any of this stuff. So ideally, someone has registered, right? And they've stayed for five minutes. So now the system knows they've been there for at least five minutes. And now it's waiting to see if they see the pitch. So let's say they don't see the pitch, right? So the answer here is no. Well, in this case, we now want to deploy another sequence, which is, hey, you left early, you need to finish what just started, go watch the webinar, here's the stuff you're gonna learn, yada, yada, yada. And again, in this email sequence, instead of sending someone to a replay where they gotta start the whole damn thing over again, you can send them to a page where they can pick up where they left off. It's really simple, like Vitalityx video player does this, Wistia's video player does this. I think like everything has that feature now, which is pick up where you left off. So it's totally not a big deal anymore, right? So now let's say that we're still looking to see if they saw the pitch and the answer is ultimately yes. You know, they saw the pitch. Well, now what you need to be able to do is wait until the end of the webinar. Cause it's, you know, or whatever you wanna call it, right? So it's never like, okay, and by the thing, see you later. Usually there's like some value stacking and the order buttons there the entire time and all that kind of stuff. So if the pitch starts at like, let's say the 50, 50 minute mark and the webinar ends at the 60 minute mark, once this happens, we need to have at least a 10 minute timer to wait here to see if they buy, right? So that's important. Okay, now, see the little star, every time there's a star, it's like an obvious thing that could be put in place. If they don't have a two step order form for their webinar or they're one to many sales process, they're just leaving money on the table, right? Most people who go to an order form, whether it's from a webinar or whatever, they don't buy. So you wanna get their name and email address first. The people who fill this thing out but don't buy, these are the single most interested people in the entire funnel. So obviously we wanna get these people so we can put them into something. That's why we got a star here. Okay, so let's say the prospect makes it to the end of the webinar or at least they see the pitch but they don't buy. Now, here's why I'm going so in depth on this. Most of the time, the thing we want the person in the funnel in this case to do, they ain't gonna do it. So let me give you an example. Most of the folks who register ain't gonna stay five minutes. That's why we need to have a sequence to get them to go back and stay five minutes. Most of the ones who stay five minutes, they ain't gonna stick around long enough to see the pitch. That's why we gotta have an email sequence to get them to go and see the pitch. Most of the ones who see the pitch, they ain't gonna buy. So we need to have something to drive them back to buy. Most of the ones who start to order aren't gonna finish ordering. So we need to have something in place to move them to that next part of the process. So it's really, really important to understand that like as complicated as this might seem, we really only have like a few stages. Did they watch five minutes? Did they see the pitch? Did they fill out step one of the order form? Did they buy? That's all. And so now we just need to have little machines move them to each little piece. So let's say they make it all the way to the end here or at least to see the pitch. And they didn't convert. Guess how many, guess what percent of people who get here ain't gonna do this? Most of them. So your client in most cases, they're just gonna send them to a replay. And that's where we should be really glad about that because we can come in and just look brilliant by doing really simple stuff. Okay, so here's the thing. If the person does not see the pitch or if they see the pitch and they don't buy, regardless, we don't wanna send them to a damn replay. We wanna send them to a more aggressive pitch which would be a video sales letter. You basically have your clients say the same thing they do in the pitch with the slightly different intro, but still making the same offer. You're just not sticking 40 minutes of content in front of it. And we wanna stick that on a webpage and we wanna put them in an email deadline sequence. So this is what's called the deadline bonus stack for a post saw pitch sequence. So this little stack here is usable with so many different sales processes. But right now I'm walking you through it from a one to many standpoint, webinar or on demand class, whatever. So they attend but don't see the pitch or they see the pitch that don't buy. Either way, we know they're interested so we might as well be following up with them aggressively. So here's what you do. You send them a deadline sequence going, hey, go buy the thing. Your ability to go buy this thing is gonna expire in two days. Have a timer on top of the page, whole nine yards. Don't worry, there's a framework for all of this kind stuff. Just giving you the big picture first. Now, most of the folks who go through this are they gonna buy? Hell no, they're not gonna buy. So first thing we gotta do is always get your two step order form. Cause man, have they filled out this step one of the two step order form? They're super interested. So they're a whole different sequence for them. But let's say they go through deadline number one and they don't convert. What you wanna do is you wanna wait for two days and now we wanna have the client put them in a second deadline. Very, actually it's totally identical email framework. The structure of the campaign is the same. The timing of the campaign is the same. All we're doing is changing the copy at the top of this webpage. Says new bonus added. Video sales letter is the same. Emails are the same except they say new bonus added and it dynamically changes the date. So driving them back to that webpage. Do they convert here? If not, we ain't gonna quit. We're gonna wait 48 hours and then we're gonna do the same thing again. In this case, webpage looks exactly the same. Video sales letter on the webpage is exactly the same. The only difference is the headline says new additional bonus added plus new payment plan. And that's what the emails in this campaign have to say. And now we look to see if they convert. So if we zoom out on this, we've now gone from, well, let's send them a replay to we've isolated every major milestone in the campaign, attend, see pitch, fill out step one. Email sequences, all of these little boxes are cut and paste email sequences that are basically saying go do something. That's all they are. So all we're doing is adding email sequences to say go do something. Chances are your client has none of this stuff. When you add this stuff, more people end up seeing the offer. They either end up finishing the webinar to see the pitch or regardless, they're seeing the offer in three different campaigns. So the entire goal is to get the offer in front of more people. So let's circle back here. Let's go back to the beginning. What do we do with them then? All right, well, chances are your client does nothing, which is great. That's what we want them to do when they meet us. So we can look really smart, but we want to like really, really help them. So always remember this fact, most people won't, all right? And what I mean by that is most people who land on the registration page ain't gonna register. Most people who register, well, they're not gonna show up. Well, if you do on demand class, typically we can change that. Most people who show up, they're not gonna see the pitch. Most people who see the pitch, they're not gonna buy. So if we know this, it really makes sense to have something in place to pick up that slack for the client, right? Cause they've gone through all the trouble to get the person registered and all that crap, or get the person on their list in the first place. All roads ultimately either end in a purchase or at this point, which I'm now gonna zoom back in on, right? So if they don't buy or show up or do whatever, they're ultimately gonna be here. And what we're gonna look for is, do they have a liquidator or not? And again, the liquidator is a little sales page that shows up after they register, but before they see the main thing they registered for. Okay, and that's just a little thing that we use to get money we would not get if we didn't do it. This is one of these things where 99% of the cases that client is gonna have more money by gluing this thing in here than if they don't do it. The only reason you wouldn't have it if for some reason the liquidator confused them. So for me in promoting Sandor, as you know, I do an on-demand class to promote the Sandor certification. I am just kicking myself right now for not having my consulting book as a liquidator after somebody opts in from a Facebook ad to go see the dang Sandor training. So dang them, hurt anything regardless. So this is like free money. So what we're gonna do is we look and if we're in a situation where we're gonna use a liquidator, which 99.99% of all situations we should be using a liquidator, right? Really trying to think of situations in which we wouldn't, there aren't many. Now what we can do is if they go through this process and they end up here where they just didn't buy, right? They either didn't attend, they didn't, or they attended but they just didn't buy after, we put them through all the other stuff. Well, here's what we can do. We can have the system check to say, hey, did they ever buy that liquidator? And if they didn't, what are we gonna do? We're gonna put a deadline sequence for the liquidator in place. Now, guess what that is? That's a bunch of cut and paste emails that lead to a sales page with a timer on it. So a deadline sequence, notice we've got, and this is critical here because it helps eliminate overwhelm. We got one, two, three, four. Five, six, seven, seven deadline sequences in this particular campaign, by the way. Now, it sounds like it's a major undertaking, doesn't it? It's not, every sequence is the same. You just change the copy a little bit but the structure and the timing and all of that stuff is the same. Like if you go back and if you're, you know, having Somnia one day and you go back and you just search for every promotional email you've gotten from me, you'll notice there's a pattern to every single one of them. Hey, there's another bonus. Hey, there's another bonus plus a payment plan. Like same thing every time. Okay, so back to work we go. I'm gonna zoom in again. So in this like perfect world, you know, we would look and we would set, like you're doing infusion soft, active campaign, pretty much any imaginable email marketing system. And then whether or not they buy the liquidator is irrelevant because our next thing is gonna be to put them in a deadline sequence for the one click offer. Wait, what's that you say? What one click offer am I talking about? Well, if you remember from last week, this right here, a liquidator is step one of an info product ascension framework, right? So if they're gonna sell something pretty cheap, well gosh damn, we might as well put some one click upsells in that sucker too, right? So naturally we wanna put these in here, okay? So if they, whether or not they buy the liquidator it doesn't matter, we're gonna wait a day or two and then if we're gonna look and we're gonna see if they bought the one click upsell and if they didn't, then we're gonna put them in a deadline sequence for that and if they did, we're gonna look and we're gonna see if they bought upsell number two and if they didn't, we're gonna put them in a deadline sequence for that and then we're gonna look and we're gonna see did they buy one click upsell number three and if they didn't, we're gonna put them in a deadline sequence for that and we're now out of stuff to sell them. So regardless of whether or not they buy one cookup cell number three, now we move them to the next campaign, right? Now, at first glance, this can seem and is overwhelming. Actually, it's not really like, I built one of these things yesterday, no kidding. I screwed something up and had to build an update thing. It's very similar to last week's checklist, incidentally. So let's bring this guy up here, okay? Oh boy, hang on a minute. Let me get us on the right slide. There we go, okay? So here's your one to many sales process checklist where we're not trying to get an appointment. And the reason I'm making this distinction, by the way, is because if they show up on the webinar, but they don't see the pitch or they see the pitch and they don't book an appointment, we follow up differently. So that's why there's a distinction and we'll address that when we start getting into the services model of businesses. But in this case, I'm just giving you this one particular process here. So we start, of course, with the big picture numbers, right? So you're sandoring out with your client and they say, well, I'm making 100 grand and I wanna make 200 grand a month. Okay, cool. And their primary thing is, you know, it's a one to many sales presentation. Or their primary thing is an ascension model and we wanna bolt one of these suckers on. Like, it doesn't matter, right? So first thing we do is we get our big picture numbers and that's how many clicks do you get? What are you spending to get them? How much money is this process making you? What's the net? Which is, hey, we just take the growth and we subtract the ad spend from it and we get the net. Same thing we did last week, you know? And now our stuff we're measuring is now slightly different. How many registrants are you getting? Assuming they're advertising, you know? So how many opt-ins for the thing, if it's a webinar or an on-demand training or whatever, are you getting? How many of them are showing up? Okay, and man, if they don't know these numbers, oh my gosh, you're so lucky. Because like, the less they know the worse it is and the better we can do for them. And so I don't mean like, you're lucky because we can pull one over on them. I mean, it's like, you've got an easy win, you know? And most people don't really know this. So then you find the monthly attendees and then how many of the attendees actually see the offer and then, you know, what's the average customer value? What's the cost to acquire the customer? And what's the net per customer? And what I left out is something so obvious that I didn't put it in here, which is how many conversions do you get, right? Which I guess you could just do, yeah. I should have had that in there. But let's just pretend this in there for now, all right? How many customers do you get? Right, so those are the big things we need to know, really, is registrants, attendees, see the pitch, buy. And then what are the costs around here? All right, and in many cases, incidentally, all the stuff we're about to go through, you might not even have to go through it because you might find out they get 10,000 registrants and they get, where are we? Only 289 of them see the pitch. Well, what do we gotta do? Just put some stuff in place to get more people to see the pitch, right? Like that's our very first thing to do, is obvious. So in many, many, many cases, the solution will present itself to you damn near instantaneously once you get these numbers because you already know how the process is supposed to work, right? You know that the more people that see the damn offer, the more money we're gonna make and if we ain't many people seeing the offer and the damn thing's still working, then hot diggity, we got big opportunity to put a whole bunch of go see the offer stuff in there. So these are the big picture numbers and then we start looking at all the stuff that they may or may not have, okay? So first thing we're gonna look at is, okay, do you have a liquidator after the registration? Now some clients just won't want to do it and I'm trying to think of a situation where it wouldn't make sense to have one and honestly I'm drawing a blank, but surely there is one. So I'm not saying like, you know, if they don't have one, they should, they have to, but if there's no reason not to, put one of these things in there, you know, for sure, right? All right, and there's of course a framework and everything for it. So number one, just check yes or no and then well, do they have an order bump on the liquidator? Now remember a liquidator offer, all it is is a cheap little front end product. So if you're gonna sell that and they buy it, the last thing you wanna do is be like, thanks for your purchase, here's the training you asked for. Oh, hell no, right? We wanna have, you know, one-click upsells and it's almost heresy, treasonous, I say. To only have one, we should at least have three, right? So do they have at least three of these things? Okay, and I think, did I do it again? Gosh dang it, I've done that however many times I did it last week too, all right? Is there exit intent pop-ups on the one-click upsells, by the way? That's when if you go to try to leave, a little pop-up will come, yes or no, okay? Is there a go-back sequence? Remember, like so I kind of, that's why I started you off with this giant sales process which really isn't that big of a deal. Do we have a contingency plan? So if they register and they don't attend, do we got something better than go watch a replay? You know, so is there a go-back sequence? And if they attend but they don't see the pitch, is there a go-finish sequence? No, let me tell you something. I'm not much of a gambler, but I'm willing to bet that you're most people that you talk to, they don't have this, all right? I mean, I'm literally like, as I'm creating this for you, I'm doing an eight-week accelerator program with five clients, ain't a one of them have these in their webinars and three, three of the five have webinars, not a single one of them. And they're all doing great, by the way, they're all making money. These aren't, you know, just fell out of bed and got started, they're all making money and doing well. You know, pretty good and nothing, none of these, none of this stuff, which is wonderful. Well, it's not for them, but it is for me because I can be like, okay, here's these three little easy things to do, right? So do they have a go-see the pitch sequence? Is there a two-step order form for the main offer? And this is nuts, right? Like if we know this to be true, if we accept it as fact that the overwhelming majority of people who go to an order form ain't gonna buy the thing, even on like a cheap little, you know, even on my book funnels, my order form is converted about 25%, which means three out of four people who go to the order form leave and they don't buy the damn book, right? So obviously we need to have one of these things, but most people won't, okay? Which is good for you. Do they have a deadline for the cart main offer cart abandon? So like what I mean is if you go to the webinar and you go to buy the main thing and you fill out name, email address and phone number, then you get the credit card part and you leave, is there a deadline sequence for that? 99 times out of 100, there's not gonna be one, thank God. Right? Is there a, I don't know why I did this here, I've got two of them, right? Is there a deadline sequence to the liquidator? Right? So if they have a liquidator and we get to the end of the line and we don't check to see if they bought it and if they didn't buy it, we don't send them a deadline sequence. What we got is an opportunity to make free money, okay? Is there a deadline sequence to the OTO? Is there a deadline sequence to the second one? Is there a deadline sequence to the third one? And most importantly, I have all of this stuff as their post webinar deadline bonus stack. That's the thing I outlined for you. That, incidentally, when I have a client that does a one to many, whether it's webinar or on demand class or whatever, even a long form video sales letter, if they don't have this, in many cases, this can get damn near double the sales of the whole campaign by adding this. Everyone who attended but didn't buy, they go through this little bonus stack. This is magic. Like, so the very first thing as your advisor, the very, very, very, very first thing is to stick one of these on there. Because that's just, you start there and then you work your way back. Because this is a huge needle mover for them, okay? This is assuming that we don't have a major issue here where nobody's attending or whatever. But most cases, they're attending, they just screw it up after that, okay? So now what we do is we get some, let's assume they've got some of these. I've never met anybody ever that's got all of these, right? So let's assume they've got some of them. We just wanna get the numbers for the some of them, right? So that's where we have this, just like last week. All right, so now what we wanna do is, okay, everything cool with the registration page conversion rate? Like sometimes, but not often, but sometimes you'll have a client where business is good and their registration page converts at like 5% and this because that's the worst registration page in history and you can double it from 5% to 10% by making a simple headline tweak and boom, huge results. Okay, and then we get like, and it's pretty rare that I ever actually go for this data. I usually, if I'm stumped somewhere, I'm like, huh, everything seems to be in place. I wonder what it could be. So like the overwhelming majority of the time. But if you need to, here are the email metrics to look at. Okay, you know, is there a problem with the go and go watch the damn thing in the first place, right? Is there a problem with go make it to five minutes? Is there a problem with the, hey, go make it to the part where you see the pitch? Is there a problem in our card abandon or our deadline stacks or our liquidator stacks anywhere? Chances are the answer here is going to be no. If there's a problem with any of these email sequences, it's going to be number of emails, not necessarily the open rate and click rate. Unless the client's just awful at email, which some of them are, right? So what we have now of course is our goo. That didn't really sound that good. Which of course stands again for glaringly obvious observations. Okay, so as your attorney and primary care physician and spiritual advisor, actually none of those statements are true. Here's what I can tell you and this is what you're going to find this as soon as you start talking to people who have this type of sales process is number one, probably, where are we? The C offer, whoops, should not have done that well. Here, I'll just stick with this. Probably this right here is broken, right? Not enough people are making it to the pitch. That's going to be thing number one. Number two, they don't have one of these. Number three, they don't have one of these. Number four, they don't have one of these. And if there's a problem with this, that means they don't have one of these. I clicked that already, can't remember. Okay, and so when you're working with them, again in Sandor, what we want to do is find the single biggest needle mover, right? So if we go back to our process, which like in a perfect world, their process would look like this. Now, obviously we're not going to expect it to be perfect. If we understand that, okay, if we get them when we're doing on-demand classes, so they're not making someone leave and wait 15 minutes, then hopefully go back and watch it. We've got this whole sequence to get them to go and at least show up and stay for five minutes. If we know that for everyone that at the very least does that, they're going to get these three deadline sequences leading to a much more aggressive video sales letter, that one thing alone is going to move the needle because more people are going to see the offer. You're going to at least double the amount of people who see the offer. And if all else remains the same, the offer itself, the conversion rate of the offer itself was unaffected by these deadlines and the additions of the bonuses and it just stayed the same. Let's just say 3% of the people who actually see the offer by, well, if we just double the amount of people that see the dang offer, which is all this thing is doing, then we're going to double the sales. And so this is typically going to be the very, very first thing that you do in this. All right, so remember in the very beginning of this video, I was like, hey, if tracking how long they stayed on the video and making sure that someone saw the pitch and then driving them back until they watch the pitch, if that seems complicated to you, just treat everybody the same, here's what I meant by that. Number one, you should really track and see if they watch the pitch and then make sure they go and finish until they watch the pitch. But if you don't want to deal with that level of technical complexity, the best thing to do is to take everybody who registers for and attends the webinar and just put them through a series of deadline sequences. So it's like, all right, the thing that I offer on the webinar is going away here and you better go buy it and just keep on running deadline sequences. And typically what I like to do is a framework that looks like this. Let's say the thing you're selling on the webinar is, I don't know, a thousand bucks. So deadline sequence number one is going to be, hey, the thing's going away, you better go here and buy it, all right? And that might last for two days. And then that thing actually does expire. Then I'll reopen it and I'll reopen it with another bonus, all right? So it's a second deadline sequence and the wording is something like, you know what? I decided to reopen this thing because I felt like it. So we don't use any of that fake scarcity crap or say the server crashed or anything like that as your show, you can reopen the damn thing if you want to, right? So I decided to reopen this offer because I felt like it. I got a lot of positive feedback and to make it better, I've decided to add a surprise bonus, but this is only available until the countdown timer hit zero. Let's say that's another two day deadline, right? And so it's again, it's a thousand dollar offer and now you've added another surprise bonus and that's a second deadline, all right? And so you let that thing expire, it needs to really expire, you know? And so if you want to keep doing that, you can reopen again a couple of days later and say, yeah, how the hell would I have decided to reopen it again because everyone's being super cool and you might have missed it. So why not? You got 48 hours, you know what I'm going to do? I've decided to add a second bonus, right? So now you've got two bonuses plus this deadline and usually I'll do it like that and then the final deadline, so we're stacking four deadline series here, right? The final deadline will be, you know what, to hell with it. You can still have all the bonuses and I'm going to reopen it for another 48 hours or whatever, but I've decided to also give you a really simple payment plan of let's see if the thing was five or if it was a thousand bucks, you could do two payments of 500 or you could do five payments of $200 or whatever and so that would be your final deadline sequence and the reason I like to do that is because I understand that in this age, attention is so fragmented, they're not reading every email and they're not checking every deadline sequence and not going, ooh, I wonder what kind of deal is going to happen now. They're missing like 90% of them because they're on every other living humans list. They're distracted by Insta Messenger, Instagram, DM, you know, the doorbell, chat messages, like everything. So you got to keep making that offer as many times as humanly possible. Now in a perfect world, you're going to do both. You're going to track them and make sure they get to the saw pitch part in the first place and then do all your crazy deadlines, but if you don't want to track them like that, then you can just, every single person who shows up, regardless of how long they stay, just pop them in the old deadline sequence and you're good to go. A lot of times I end up doing that because I don't feel like doing the tech stuff. So that's a little bit of, that's a little bit of cheat sheet stuff for you. Right there, okay? So that's how to maximize a one too many pitch, whether it's a live webinar, whether it's an automated webinar in one of those services or whether it's an on-demand class, which is quickly becoming my personal favorite. That's just opt in and watch the video now. Doesn't matter, all the same, really. Whole process works exactly the same. So get out there and maximize your sales process. Get yourself some money. I'll see you in the next video.
